lesson.learn: Mass extinctions are not just endings; they are restructuring events that wipe out some lineages and give others room to expand. When a catastrophe removes dominant species, it opens ecological space for survivors, and those survivors often diversify into roles that were previously occupied by more successful competitors. That is why extinction events matter so much in evolutionary history: they do not simply reduce life, they redirect it. The end-Cretaceous asteroid impact is the most famous example, but it is part of a longer pattern in which crisis creates new evolutionary opportunities.
lesson.simpler: Extinction events change evolution by clearing out ecosystems and leaving empty niches behind. The species that survive can then spread into those open roles and evolve in new directions.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Extinction is destructive, but it is also creative at the scale of deep time, because the survivors inherit a reshaped world and evolve into the spaces left behind.
lesson.deeperDive: The fossil record shows that evolution often moves in pulses separated by shocks. The end-Permian extinction, the end-Triassic event, and the end-Cretaceous impact all reset ecosystems in different ways, but the pattern is similar: the diversity of life drops sharply, then rebounds through new forms. Some lineages vanish completely, while others survive because they are small, adaptable, widespread, or able to feed on unusual resources. This is why evolutionary history is not a smooth ladder. It is closer to a landscape constantly reshaped by earthquakes. A mass extinction can favor generalists over specialists, small bodies over large ones, or organisms with flexible diets over those tied to narrow food webs. In that sense, extinction is one of the strongest forces in evolution because it changes what kinds of traits matter most. The survivors do not just continue life; they inherit the future.
lesson.funFact: After a mass extinction, some of the most successful species are often the ones that once looked too ordinary to matter.
references: Smithsonian and NASA resources on mass extinctions and the Chicxulub impact.
University geology references on the K-Pg boundary and evolutionary turnover.
Review literature on extinction selectivity and recovery ecosystems.
Wikimedia Commons crater and impact-image file pages.

people: Walter Alvarez - Geologist associated with the asteroid-impact explanation for the end-Cretaceous extinction
Luis Alvarez - Nobel Prize-winning physicist who helped identify the iridium signal in extinction rocks
Jan Smit - Researcher who studied impact evidence around the K-Pg boundary
Gerta Keller - Geologist known for work on extinction timing and cause debates
places: Chicxulub crater, Mexico - Impact site linked to the end-Cretaceous extinction
K-Pg boundary layers - Rock layers that record the extinction and recovery transition
Permian-Triassic boundary sites - Areas that preserve the largest extinction event in Earth history
Fossil basins worldwide - Places where turnover after crisis can be measured
years: 252 million years ago - End-Permian mass extinction reshapes life on Earth
201 million years ago - End-Triassic crisis clears the way for dinosaur expansion
66 million years ago - Chicxulub impact triggers the end-Cretaceous extinction
Modern era - Extinction science links geology, ecology, and evolutionary recovery
concepts: Ecological niche - A role in the ecosystem that can be vacated and refilled
Recovery radiation - Rapid diversification after a major die-off
Selectivity - The fact that some traits improve survival during crises
Catastrophic reset - A sudden change that alters evolutionary competition
Boundary layer - A rock layer that marks a major time transition in the fossil record
